text.fullText: I am writing in support of the EPAs proposed revisions to the CWA Section 401 Water Quality Certification regulations. As a life-long resident and student of Virginia, studying food science technology and agribusiness, I care deeply about both environmental conservation and responsible economic development. In Virginia agriculture, infrastructure, and natural resources are deeply connected, so regulatory clarity is not simply a legal issue but also one that can affect the lives of the many farmers and rural communities that call Virginia home. The authority that Section 401 places on states and authorized Tribes to ensure compliance with water standards is essential. For my state in particular, the river’s, streams, and Chesapeake Bay are not just culturally significant but essential to our economy. Farmers rely on it for livestock and rural communities depend on it for drinking water. Protecting water quality is not optional, it is foundational. Effective environmental protection such as the Chesapeake Bay TMDL depend on clear and enforceable regulatory frameworks. Virginia’s participation in the Chesapeake Bay Total Maximum Daily Load program serves as a great example on how structured and science based limits can drive measurable water quality improvements. The Chesapeake Bay TMDL, established by the EPA in 2010 under the CWA, set specific nutrient and sediment reduction targets for states within the watershed. Virginia has since implemented watershed implementation plans that balance agricultural productivity with pollution reduction requirements. That effort leads to show that environmental progress is strongest when responsibilities are clearly defined and grounded in statutory authority. The Supreme Court has clarified that states may impose conditions related to water quality impacts as recognized in Public Utility District No.1 of Jefferson County v. Washington Department of Ecology (511 U.S 700 (1994)). Additionally it is written that the authority granted under Section 401 is not unlimited and is specifically tied to water quality impacts associated with discharges. However over time, interpretation of this authority has expanded in ways that have created opportunities for uncertainty for individuals and agencies. It’s important that environmental protection must remain strong, however regulatory overreach can unintentionally undermine long-term conservation goals. This concern stems from some states in recent years using the Section 401 certification to address broader concerns beyond water compliance tied to discharge, including general climate policy considerations or entire project objections. While those concerns still may be important public policy issues, they are not necessarily within the scope of authority granted under Section 401. When certification decisions extend beyond statutory boundaries such as this, I believe it contributes to unnecessary delays and legal disputes over projects. In Virginia and I’m sure many other states as well, this kind of uncertainty has real consequences. Agricultural operations often depend on infrastructure such as irrigation systems, processing facilities, transportation networks, and energy access. Renewable energy projects, which many support as part of long-term environmental strategy, also require permits that may trigger Section 401 review. If the certification process becomes unpredictable or inconsistent, investment slows. Rural communities that already operate on thin margins will unfortunately feel that impact first. I’d like to now directly reference some revisions proposed and my position, starting with the proposed revisions to clarify the scope of certification at 40 CFR 121.3 I see as absolutely necessary. Clear boundaries don’t weaken environmental protections; they strengthen them. When certifying authorities focus solely on ensuring compliance with water quality standards, their decisions are more defensible and therefore much more effective. Strong environmental policy additionally depends on legal durability. If agencies act beyond statutory limits, even with good intentions, they risk litigation that can delay environmental progress. I additionally support the proposed revisions to the contents of a request for certification (40 CFR 121.5) and the contents of a certification decision (40 CFR 121.7). Transparency is critical; farmers, developers, and local governments need to understand what information is required and what standards are being applied. Clear documentation requirements increase accountability and allow stakeholders to better prepare themselves for compliance. In agriculture especially, planning and timing are everything when it comes to success. Regulatory clarity helps producers make informed decisions rather than operate in uncertainty. Additionally, clarifying the rules surrounding withdrawal and resubmittal of certification requests at 40 CFR 121.6 addresses a procedural issue that has created confusion. The CWA provides that; if a state fails or refuses to act within a reasonable period of time, not to exceed one year, certification is waived (33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1)). Clear guidance on how timelines function ensures that statutory deadlines are meaningful while still allowing appropriate environmental review. Predictable timelines rather than those variably deemed “reasonable” are essential for infrastructure and agricultural investments that depend on financing and seasonal cycles. I would finally like to state that supporting these revisions does not mean prioritizing development over conservation, I personally believe the opposite is true. Sustainable development requires strong environmental safeguards that are consistently and lawfully applied. When regulatory authority is clearly defined, states can then focus their expertise on efforts surrounding protecting water quality rather than engaging in broader policy debates that belong in legislative arenas. The CWA was designed to empower states while maintaining federal structure and limits. By revising the 2023 Rule to better align with statutory text and Supreme Court precedent, the EPA is reinforcing that cooperative balance it was created for. Certifying authorities bring a very meaningful power to protect water quality while simultaneously applicants gain more clear expectations, and federal agencies additionally benefit from improved efficiency and predictability in a sector that is already filled with so many unpredictable variables. Therefore as someone who cares deeply about both Virginia’s agricultural communities and its natural resources, I support this proposed rule. Clear statutory boundaries, transparent procedures, and predictable timelines will ultimately protect water quality while enabling responsible economic growth. This balance is essential for rural communities, environmental conservation, and long-term sustainability. Clean Water Act, 33 U.S.C. § 1341.
